text: The laparoscopic camera provides a panoramic view of the abdominal cavity. The surgeon places a right-hand port and a left-hand port, followed by a 5mm port for a liver retractor, which is used to retract the liver away from the upper pole of the right kidney.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the ascending colon from the anterior surface of the right kidney, performing medial mobilization of the mesocolon to expose retroperitoneal structures. The duodenum is exposed, and caucarization is initiated. The inferior vena cava is visible just medial to the right kidney, and the hepatorenal ligaments attached to the superior pole of the right kidney are cut.

The surgeon opens the Gerota's fascia near the lower pole of the right kidney, identifying and dissecting the ureter in this region. A 5mm assistant port is placed to retract the lower pole of the kidney laterally, allowing for gradual lifting of the kidney.

The surgeon performs a layer-wise dissection of the hilar tissue, identifying and dissecting an accessory renal vein, which is clipped and cut to facilitate the exposure of the right renal vein. The inferior and superior borders of the renal vein are dissected, and a higher lymph node is identified. The ureter is clipped and cut, aiding in the elevation of the lower pole of the kidney.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the renal artery, clipping it on both sides before cutting it. A plane is developed between the posterior surface of the renal vein and the surrounding hilar tissue. The kidney is retracted laterally, and Hem-o-lok clips are applied sequentially on the renal vein, which is then cut between the clips. The remaining hilar lymphovascular tissue is clipped and cut, allowing for gradual mobilization of the kidney.
The detached kidney is visible, ready for removal from the body. The surgical field appears dry, and a drain is placed in the high lump area.
